channel.title: TTP - Critical Reasoning on Arnesh's Website
channel.description: Recent content in TTP - Critical Reasoning on Arnesh's Website

item.title: 1.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions. 💡MUST KNOW: In the verbal reasoning section of the GMAT, between 9 and 13 of the 36 questions that appear will be Critical Reasoning questions. Critical Reasoning questions fall into the following eleven major question types: (1) Identify the Assumption These questions ask us to identify an assumption upon which an argument depends.

item.title: 1.2 The Basic Layout of Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, which is usually, though not always, in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer. 💡MUST KNOW: Each Critical Reasoning question begins with a written stimulus, usually in the form of a short passage of about 100 words or fewer. Accompanying the stimulus is a question and five answer choices. The question will always ask us to determine which of the five answer choices is logically related to the stimulus in a particular way.

item.title: 1.3 The Skills That Critical Reasoning Questions Test
item.description: Lesson Content Many students lack sufficient understanding of what GMAT Critical Reasoning questions test; they don’t understand the skills required to perform well on these questions. GMAT Critical Reasoning questions are designed to test our skill in making, analyzing, and evaluating arguments and plans. 💡MUST KNOW: GMAT Critical Reasoning questions are designed to test our skill in making, analyzing, and evaluating arguments and plans. Correctly answering these questions requires using what the makers of the GMAT call higher-order thinking skills to reason your way to the correct choice.

item.title: 1.4 The Structure of an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content Most GMAT Critical reasoning questions are based on arguments. 💡MUST KNOW: An argument is a series of statements that logically explain a point of view. In order to effectively answer Critical Reasoning questions, we must understand how arguments are commonly structured. The Conclusion The Evidence The Assumptions The Background Information Let’s begin by discussing the conclusion.

item.title: 1.5 The Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view. An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion. 💡MUST KNOW: The conclusion of the argument is the sentence that states the argument’s point of view. An author’s conclusion is always a statement of opinion. In other words, whenever an author sets forth a conclusion, she’s presenting what she believes to be true.

item.title: 1.6 Evidence or Premises
item.description: Lesson Content Evidence or premises are facts that support the conclusion and are used to build the argument. Let’s consider our simple example again: Critical Reasoning is like a game. Games are fun. Critical Reasoning must be fun. In the argument above, the premises that support the conclusion are “Critical Reasoning is like a game,” and “Games are fun.” Those statements support the conclusion “Critical Reasoning must be fun.” Evidence provided by authors of Critical Reasoning questions comes in many forms.

item.title: 1.7 Background Information
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes arguments contain background information that the author uses to frame the argument. 💡MUST KNOW: An author presents us with background information to “get us up to speed” on anything we may need to know in order to effectively understand the argument. For example, in the following passage, the author uses the first sentence to convey background information to provide us with enough context to be able to completely understand the argument:

item.title: 1.8 Assumptions
item.description: Lesson Content A vital part of any argument is its assumptions. An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of an argument is to work. 💡MUST KNOW: An assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true if the logic of the argument is to work. It can be helpful to think of an argument as a math problem: (any background info)+ Evidence + Assumptions = Conclusion

item.title: 1.9 Some Essential Words to Understand
item.description: Lesson Content In this chapter, we’re helping you build a basic toolkit. “A few” means “not many, but more than one.” “Several” means “three or more but not many.” “Some” means “more than one, but possibly all.” “Many” means “a large number of.” Notice that “many” is a version of “some.” Notice also that “many” is not necessarily “most.” “Most” means “more than 50 percent.” “A majority” means “more than 50 percent.

item.title: 1.10 When Answering a Critical Reasoning Question, Be Sure to Carefully Evaluate All Five of the Answer Choices
item.description: Lesson Content When answering a Critical Reasoning question, even if we think that we have found the correct answer, we should go through all the five choices before choosing a correct answer. 💡MUST KNOW: Even if we think that we have found the correct answer, we should go through all the five options before choosing a correct answer. Notice that Critical Reasoning question stems ask us to find the “best” choice among the five choices provided.

item.title: 1.11 What to Do If you Have Eliminated All of the Answer Choices
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes we find that we have eliminated all the answer choices. 💡MUST KNOW: If you have eliminated all the answer choices, simply take a moment to be sure that you have properly understood the passage. Then go back through the answer choices and carefully reevaluate them. This time through you need to really bear down on each answer choice and rigorously evaluate its merits. By continuing to consider the differences between the choices and by going back to the passage to find details that you missed in previous readings, you’ll find that one choice that is the correct answer to the question.

item.title: 1.12 To Do Well in Critical Reasoning, See the Logical Implications of Statements
item.description: Lesson Content To do well in Critical Reasoning, we must see the logical implications of statements in Critical Reasoning passages and answer choices. A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions. 💡MUST KNOW: A logical implication is simply the logical consequence of a set of facts or conditions. We can present a logical implication with an “if/then” statement. In the above “if/then” statement, Y is the logical implication of X.

item.title: 1.13 Do Not Attempt to “Pre-Think” the Answers to Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Many students have learned a gimmicky strategy for answering Critical Reasoning questions: read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be. Let’s make this very simple – do not pre-think. 💡MUST KNOW: Do not “pre-think” answers by attempting to read the question passage, read the question stem, and then “pre-think” what the correct answer will be. Doing so will likely limit your ability to become skilled at answering Critical Reasoning questions.

item.title: 1.14 Do Not Eliminate or Choose a Critical Reasoning Answer Choice Because of the Presence of One Word
item.description: Lesson Content Since a single word in a Critical Reasoning answer choice can have a significant effect on the meaning conveyed by that choice, people often get the impression that it makes sense to eliminate or choose Critical Reasoning answer choices because of the presence of particular words in the choices. For instance, since answer choices that use words such as “all,” “none,” “always,” or “never” state things that are extreme, people often use the strategy of eliminating any choice that uses such a word, terming such choices “extreme choices.

item.title: 1.15 Do Not Skip Words When Reading Critical Reasoning Passages, Question Stems, or Answer Choices in an Attempt to Save Time.
item.description: Lesson Content Often, because they want to save time, or simply because they don’t feel like reading every word, test-takers skip or gloss over words in Critical Reasoning passages, question stems, or answer choices or decide not to read the last several words of a passage, question stem, or answer choice. In fact, what often happens is that a test-taker who has skipped words in a Critical Reasoning question flails around and repeatedly goes through the answer choices without finding the correct answer because, in skipping those words, the test-taker has failed to acquire the information necessary for answering the question.

item.title: 1.16 Do Not Eliminate an Answer Choice Just Because it Brings in New Information.
item.description: Lesson Content Many people get the impression that they should robotically eliminate any Critical Reasoning answer choice that brings in new information that is different from what is discussed in the passage. “The author never said anything about ants in the argument. ” “This answer choice brings in new information. ” “What does the Mediterranean Sea have to do with Lake Michigan?” “This answer choice seems off-topic. ” “This answer choice came out of nowhere.

item.title: 1.17 To Improve Your Critical Reasoning Performance, You Must Discover and Then Address Habits That Lead to Choosing Incorrect Answers
item.description: Lesson Content Test-takers often miss Critical Reasoning questions for reasons that were predictable and avoidable. 💡MUST KNOW: A key aspect of improving Critical Reasoning performance is discovering and then addressing habits that lead to choosing incorrect answers. Look at the below list of common reasons people miss Critical Reasoning questions that they could have gotten correct. I missed important details in the passage. I failed to read the entire passage.

item.title: 1.18 We Don’t Need Specialized, Outside Knowledge to Answer Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge. 💡MUST KNOW: Correctly answering Critical Reasoning questions does not require any outside, specialized knowledge. Any outside information necessary for answering these questions is considered “common knowledge” among people with common sense and a basic level of education. In other words, yes, we will encounter passages on economics, science, music theory, politics, and psychology, to name a few topics, but any outside information we’ll need to answer Critical Reasoning questions will be information that is considered generally known, and widely accepted, by the general population.

item.title: 1.19 Timing and Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The verbal section of the GMAT allots 65 minutes for answering 36 questions, or an average time of 1.8 minutes per question (about 1 minute and 48 seconds per question). Of course, if you’ll need to average 1.8 minutes per question and yet may spend 2 minutes per Critical Reasoning question, where will the extra time come from? In general, Sentence Corrections take less time, on average, than Critical Reasoning questions.

item.title: 1.20 Critical Reasoning Questions Have Been Consistent Over Time
item.description: Lesson Content The GMAT is a standardized test, and so, the test-makers seek to make each exam more or less consistent with previous exams so that scores can be meaningfully compared. OK! We’re ready to get started with our first major chapter: Identify the Assumption.

item.title: 2.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Many Critical Reasoning questions require that we determine what information is missing from the arguments presented to us. 💡MUST KNOW: The ability to determine what information is missing from arguments is of significant importance to success in answering Critical Reasoning questions. We’ll elaborate upon this point as the course proceeds, but we are about to see this important point front and center with assumptions.

item.title: 2.2 Assumptions: Unstated Pieces of Information that Must Be True in Order for Arguments to Work
item.description: Lesson Content As we discussed in the previous chapter, an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the logic of an argument to work. To see more clearly how assumptions function in arguments, let’s consider a simple argument: Dogs like to bark. Therefore, Fido must like to bark. Notice that we have one premise: Dogs like to bark. Given that premise, the author concludes the following:

item.title: 2.3 Recognizing an Identify the Assumption Question
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing an Identify the Assumption question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language to ask for answer choices that fill logical gaps in arguments. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends? Which of the following is an assumption made in drawing the conclusion above? The argument depends on which of the following? The conclusion drawn above is based on the assumption that _______.

item.title: 2.4 Strategy for Identify the Assumption Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To answer Assumption questions correctly, follow this 6-step strategy. Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage. The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to an Assumption question is understanding the passage. The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized. So, your first job in answering an Assumption question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.

item.title: 2.5 In Critical Reasoning Questions, Authors Assume that their Arguments are Correct.
item.description: Lesson Content In Critical Reasoning, we use the term “author” to refer to whoever is setting forth an argument in a question. The “authors” of these arguments vary from question to question, but the one commonality that these authors all share is that they assume that they are correct, whatever their arguments may be. 💡MUST KNOW: In making the arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions, the authors of these arguments assume that they are correct; they assume that their arguments are sound and logical.

item.title: 2.6 Common Ways in which Assumption Question Correct Answers Work
item.description: Lesson Content The correct answer to an assumption question will always fill a gap in an argument.

item.title: 2.6.1 Assumption Correct Answer Type 1: States an Assumption that there is no Offsetting Factor
item.description: Lesson Content When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid. 💡MUST KNOW: When an author sets forth an argument, plan, proposal, or course of action, she assumes that there will be no offsetting factors that will somehow render that argument, plan, proposal, or course of action invalid.

item.title: 2.6.2 Assumption Correct Answer Type 2: States an Assumption that Must be True in order for a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion to be Correct
item.description: Lesson Content Many arguments that appear in the GMAT involve cause-and-effect reasoning, something that we’ll often mention in the chapters that follow, but we’ll begin building our expertise in cause-and-effect reasoning here. An author makes a cause-and-effect claim when she maintains that some factor x is the cause of some outcome y. When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.

item.title: 2.6.3 Assumption Correct Answer Type 3: States an Assumption that There are No Other Paths to Success
item.description: Lesson Content In Assumption questions, we often encounter arguments that conclude that some plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur. However, the evidence that she provides will not be sufficient alone for the argument to work. 💡MUST KNOW: Whenever an author concludes that a plan will not work or that some outcome will not occur, she must assume that there is not some other way or some other path (that she has failed to consider) via which the plan could work or via which the outcome could occur.

item.title: 2.6.4 Assumption Correct Answer Type 4: Highlights an Assumption that a Plan for Solving a Problem will not Result in the Same Problem or a Similar One
item.description: Lesson Content As we now know, many arguments in the GMAT focus on plans or courses of action. Another way of thinking about this is that, when an author sets forth a plan or course of action, she believes that she has correctly and completely analyzed whatever situation the plan or course of action centers on. So then, in Assumption questions, authors typically make the assumption that a plan or a course of action they suggest for solving a problem will not itself result in the same problem in a new way.

item.title: 2.6.5 Assumption Correct Answer Type 5: An Assumption that Something that is Likely True is Actually True
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes in an Assumption question, an author will base her argument on an idea that is probably true but is not guaranteed to be true. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, an argument in an Assumptions question depends on the assumption that something that is likely true is actually true. Consider the following example: Three years ago, some of the students attending Rockville High School began for the first-time attending weekend classes designed to help them pass a state-wide assessment test.

item.title: 2.6.6 Assumption Correct Answer Type 6: Highlights an Assumption that a Change Occurring over Time does not Render the Conclusion Invalid
item.description: Lesson Content Arguments about topics such as plans, predictions, or explanations can include elements that are from different time periods. What all of these arguments have in common are assumptions that a conclusion about something occurring in one time period can be logically based on evidence derived from events in another time period. For instance, in arriving at a conclusion about events that occurred a thousand years ago by observing certain conditions that exist today, one may be assuming that the relevant conditions that exist today are similar to conditions that existed a thousand years ago.

item.title: 2.7 Assumptions Can Be Inconspicuous
item.description: Lesson Content One thing that often surprises people is how inconspicuous assumptions can be. For example: Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year. Let’s look at the structure of the argument: Premise: Henrietta’s apples pies are the best apple pies.

item.title: 2.8 Working with Tricky Question Stems That Contain Limiting Words
item.description: Lesson Content Assumption questions will sometimes include in their question stems the word “except” or “not.” The use of question stems that use such words can make questions a bit more challenging. The following two question stems are examples of Assumption question stems that contain “except” or “not”: The logic of the argument above depends on all of the following EXCEPT: Which of the following is not an assumption on which the argument depends?

item.title: 2.9 The Negation Technique
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, you know well that an assumption is an unstated piece of information that must be true in order for the given evidence to lead to the conclusion. After all, what would happen to the argument if we were to discover that there WERE other ways in which a person could become exposed to Compound X? In such a case, the argument would fall apart – right?

item.title: 2.10 The General Differences Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Assumption Questions
item.description: Lesson Content A key aspect of arriving at the correct answers to Assumption questions is noticing the difference between trap choices and correct answers. It says something that is rather clearly not an assumption integral to the argument but is so obviously connected to the argument that a test-taker is tempted to choose it. It says something that may seem to be an assumption integral to the argument but is not actually an assumption integral to the argument.

item.title: 2.11 Common Incorrect Answer Choices in Assumption Questions
item.description: Lesson Content In the last section, we discussed how correct answers generally differ from trap choices. 💡MUST KNOW: Knowing the most common types of incorrect choices that appear in Assumption questions can help us considerably. Let’s begin by discussing an answer choice that supports the wrong conclusion.

item.title: 2.11.1 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 1: Supports the Wrong Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing. 💡MUST KNOW: Sometimes, Critical Reasoning incorrect answer choices will take important words or concepts from the stimulus and swap these with seemingly similar stand-ins that do not actually mean the same thing. Of course, since what these incorrect choices discuss is similar to what the passage discusses, if we’re not paying attention, it’s easy to become ensnared by such a switch.

item.title: 2.11.2 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 2: Choices that Do the Reverse of What the Question Asks For
item.description: Lesson Content Assumption questions often include answer choices that do basically the reverse of what a correct answer would do. These “reverse” answer choices can be surprisingly seductive at first since they express something that is relevant to the argument in the passage and that has an effect on that argument. Let’s return to Steven and his unfortunate experience with the polygraph test: Steven was asked to take a polygraph, i.

item.title: 2.11.3 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 3: Explains an Aspect of the Scenario Presented in the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes an incorrect choice in an Assumption question will provide an explanation for something discussed in the passage. Let’s consider again our example of Henrietta and her pie: Henrietta bakes the best apple pies in Clay County. The best apple pie entries win the Clay County Southern Charm baking contest every year without fail. Therefore, Henrietta’s apple pie will win the Southern Charm baking contest this year. Which of the following is an assumption upon which the argument depends?

item.title: 2.11.4 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 4: A Statement that is a Conclusion Supported by what is Said in the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content Another type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is a choice that states a conclusion that is supported by the passage rather than an assumption upon which the argument depends. 💡MUST KNOW: When answering an Assumption question, be careful not to choose an answer choice that states a conclusion supported by what the passage says. We must find a choice that expresses an assumption integral to the argument.

item.title: 2.11.5 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 5: Mentions a Better, or Alternative, Plan
item.description: Lesson Content Often, in an Assumption question, the argument will be about a plan, and often the conclusion of the argument in such a question is essentially that the plan will work. In many such questions, one of the incorrect answer choices will mention a plan that is an alternative to the plan the argument concludes will work, and often this alternative plan will appear to be better than the plan that the argument is promoting.

item.title: 2.11.6 Assumption Incorrect Choice Type 6: Supports the Conclusion but is not an Assumption Upon Which the Argument Depends
item.description: Lesson Content A tricky type of incorrect answer choice that can appear in an Assumption question is one that supports the conclusion but is not an assumption upon which the argument depends. To avoid choosing such an incorrect choice, we have to keep in mind that providing support for a conclusion and being an assumption upon which an argument depends are not necessarily the same thing. To see how this type of incorrect choice can work, consider the following example.

item.title: 2.12 Assumptions and Weakening an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content Remember, when an author makes an argument, she assumes that that argument is correct. In the next chapter, we’ll discuss ways to weaken an argument. Before we get there, we want you to see that an argument’s assumptions are closely tied to statements that could weaken that argument. Consider the following mini-example: It tends to be the case that the higher the quality of the food a person eats, the better that person’s health will be.

item.title: 3.2 Focus on Assumptions to Effectively Weaken an Argument.
item.description: Lesson Content As we’ve discussed, an argument is composed of a conclusion, the evidence that supports the conclusion, any background information, and the assumptions that connect the evidence with the conclusion. Conclusion Evidence Background Assumptions On the GMAT, we’ll never find an answer choice that weakens an argument by attacking the premises (that is, the evidence) upon which the conclusion rests, because while in real life premises may turn out to be invalid, on the GMAT the premises are taken to be true.

item.title: 3.3 Spotting a Weaken the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Weaken the Argument question is easy, because these questions utilize predictable language. Which of the following, if true, most seriously weakens the argument above? Which of the following, if true, casts the most doubt on the conclusion drawn above? Which of the following, if true, does the most to call into question the claim made? Which of the following, if discovered, would be evidence against the speculation above?

item.title: 3.4 Strategy for Weaken the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Weaken question, follow this 6-step strategy. Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage. The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Weaken question is understanding the passage. The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized. So, your first job in answering a Weaken question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.

item.title: 3.5 Weaken Does Not Mean Destroy.
item.description: Lesson Content When we hear the phrase “weaken the argument,” we may feel that our job is to destroy the argument. In fact, much of the time, the correct answer to a Weaken question, especially a more difficult one, will not provide an earth-shattering attack. Thus, we need to be adept at finding subtle ways to weaken an argument. 💡MUST KNOW: Often, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will not totally destroy the argument but instead weaken it in a subtle yet important way.

item.title: 3.6 New Topics in Weaken Question Answer Choices
item.description: Lesson Content Students often get the impression that, in answering Weaken the Argument questions, they should eliminate any answer choice that seems to bring up a new topic. The truth is, however, that all correct answers to Weaken the Argument questions will bring up new information, and it’s important to understand that information that does not resemble the information that makes up the argument is not the same as irrelevant information.

item.title: 3.7 Common Ways in which Correct Answers to Weaken the Argument Questions Weaken Arguments
item.description: Lesson Content The ways in which correct answers weaken arguments are predictable. As we go through these answer types, there are, at least, three things that you can accomplish. • You can learn about the types of answers discussed, any of which could easily show up in Critical Reasoning questions on the GMAT. • You can note the logic that underlies each answer type, as that logic could show up in Weaken questions or in questions of other types.

item.title: 3.7.1 Weakening Answer Type 1: Exposes a False Dichotomy by Offering a Third Alternative
item.description: Lesson Content One way in which authors of arguments fail to consider other possibilities is by relying on false dichotomies in coming to conclusions. The term “false dichotomy” may sound complex, but what it means is actually rather simple. 💡MUST KNOW: A false dichotomy occurs when an argument assumes that, just because one conclusion is impossible, another conclusion must be true. In other words, a false dichotomy occurs when an author incorrectly limits the number of possible conclusions to two.

item.title: 3.7.2 Weakening Answer Type 2: An Answer that Calls into Question a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content We’ve learned a bit about cause-and-effect arguments in the Assumptions chapter. We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be difficult. 💡MUST KNOW: We must be careful whenever a speaker makes a cause-and-effect argument, because determining whether one thing actually causes another can be very difficult. In fact, making such determinations is so difficult that, for decades, courts of law did not acknowledge that cigarette smoking causes lung cancer.

item.title: 3.7.3 Weakening Answer Type 3: Shows that a Generalization that Supports an Argument is Faulty
item.description: Lesson Content When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion. 💡MUST KNOW: When an author uses a sample of a population as evidence in his argument, he assumes that that sample provides solid support for his conclusion. In Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption can be a poor one. Authors sometimes make generalizations, drawing conclusions about populations by considering small samples of those populations.

item.title: 3.7.4 Weakening Answer Type 4: Shows that a Comparison Used to Support a Conclusion is Faulty
item.description: Lesson Content When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion. 💡MUST KNOW: When an author uses a comparison as evidence in an argument, she assumes that that comparison provides solid support for her conclusion. Often, in Weaken the Argument questions, we’ll see that that assumption is a poor one, because authors often make faulty comparisons. When an author of an argument makes a faulty comparison, she attempts to support the argument by comparing two things that are fundamentally different in an important way such that the comparison does not actually support the conclusion.

item.title: 3.7.5 Weakening Answer Type 5: Indicates that, in Arriving at the Conclusion, the Author Confused Numbers and Percentages
item.description: Lesson Content When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data. 💡MUST KNOW: When an author presents as evidence statistical data, such as numbers and percentages, she assumes that (1) that data provides solid support for her conclusion and (2) that she has properly analyzed that data.

item.title: 3.7.6 Weakening Answer Type 6: Brings Up New Information that Indicates that a Plan Won’t Work
item.description: Lesson Content Remember, any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that the plan will work. So, when an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no malicious variables. 💡MUST KNOW: Any time an author presents a plan, she assumes that there are no variables that will enter into the equation that will spoil the plan. In Weaken the Argument questions, this assumption often turns out to be a poor one.

item.title: 3.7.7 Weakening Answer Type 7: Indicates that a Plan that the Argument Concludes will not Work Actually will Work
item.description: Lesson Content Plans are common topics in GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, and Weaken questions can involve conclusions that plans will not work or don’t make sense. 💡MUST KNOW: When authors claim that plans will not work or don’t make sense, support for such conclusions can be weakened by correct answers that provide new information that indicates that actually the plans will work or do make sense. The correct answers to these questions generally weaken the arguments by providing new information that offsets or makes up for the information used to support the arguments’ conclusions that the plans will not work.

item.title: 3.8 The Difference Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Weaken Questions
item.description: Lesson Content One key to correctly answering Weaken questions is being able to see the difference between trap choices and correct answers. Trap Choice: In general, a trap choice in a Weaken the Argument question says something that seems detrimental to the conclusion and that is somehow related to the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that the choice actually weakens the argument.

item.title: 3.9 Common Incorrect Answer Choices in Weaken Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Forewarned is forearmed. Let’s first look at the type of answer choice that strengthens rather than weakens the argument.

item.title: 3.9.1 Strengtheners - Choices That Are the Opposite of the Correct Answer
item.description: Lesson Content At this point, you understand that a Weaken the Argument question asks us to find a choice that does just that. Don’t think you’d ever select an answer choice that strengthens an argument when you’re being asked to weaken it? Don’t be so sure; these opposite answer choices can be seductive. Consider the following sample conclusion and strengthening answer choice: Conclusion: Clearly, eating bananas is the key move that resulted in powerlifter’s strength almost doubling last year.

item.title: 3.9.2 Choices That are Pseudo-Weakeners
item.description: Lesson Content In a Weaken question, along with the correct answer choice, which actually weakens the argument, there can be one or more pseudo-weakener answer choices, which may give the impression that they weaken the argument but don’t really get the job done. Consider the following example: The football coach at Verdantville high school wants the players on the team to gain weight. So, he is providing the players with all the chocolate bars that they can eat.

item.title: 3.9.3 Frameshifts – Choices that Weaken the Support for the Wrong Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content Consider an argument that supports Conclusion X. When a frameshift occurs, an incorrect answer choice will cast doubt on a conclusion that is related to or very similar to Conclusion X but is not Conclusion X. If we’re not careful, it’s quite easy to fall for a frameshift because of the similarity between the actual conclusion of the argument and the conclusion called into question by the incorrect choice.

item.title: 3.9.4 Pertinent but Incorrect Choices
item.description: Lesson Content Some of the most tempting incorrect choices in Weaken questions are choices that mention things that one would likely consider in a real-world review of a situation. At the same time, while these answer choices may say things worthy of consideration, they do not weaken the arguments that they follow. 💡MUST KNOW: We must be careful not to be tempted into choosing an answer choice that says something that is valid and is pertinent to what the passage discusses but does not affect the argument.

item.title: 3.9.5 Answer Choices That Appear to Attack Premises
item.description: Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to a Weaken the Argument question will never be one that attacks a premise of the argument, because premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined. 💡MUST KNOW: Premises in GMAT arguments are taken to be true and therefore cannot be undermined. Therefore, an answer choice that says something that appears to undermine a premise will not weaken the argument.

item.title: 3.10 Tricky Weaken Question Stems: EXCEPT and NOT
item.description: Lesson Content Weaken the Argument questions will sometimes include the word “except” or “not” in their question stems to make the questions a bit more challenging. Any of the following, if true, undermines support for the speaker’s claim EXCEPT: Which of the following, if true, does not weaken the argument above? When a Weaken question has the word “except” or “not” in the stem, four out of the five answer choices will weaken the argument.

item.title: 4.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions are among the most common types of GMAT Critical Reasoning Questions. The good news is that, as promised, the skills that you learned in the Assumption Chapter – and reinforced in the Weaken the Argument Chapter – will be significant assets to you as you learn to handle Strengthen the Argument questions. Let’s begin discussing how to effectively strengthen an argument.

item.title: 4.2 Understanding How to Effectively Strengthen an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content As just mentioned, strengthening an argument involves providing new information that strengthens the support for the argument’s conclusion. To see strengthening in action, let’s consider an example: Alice knows all about making smoothies, and she makes smoothies that all her friends like. Clearly, her new smoothie shop will be a success. We can strengthen that argument by providing new information that makes the case for the conclusion more convincing.

item.title: 4.3 Recognizing a Strengthen the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Most of the time, recognizing a Strengthen the Argument question is easy because these questions utilize predictable language. Which of the following, if true, most strongly supports _____? Which of the following, if true, provides the strongest grounds for _____? Which of the following, if true, most helps to justify _____? Which of the following, if true, provides the best reason for _____? Which of the following, if true, would strengthen _____?

item.title: 4.4 Strategy for Strengthen the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To find the correct answer to a Strengthen question, follow this 6-step strategy. Step 1 – Carefully read and understand the passage. The foundation of arriving at the correct answer to a Strengthen question is understanding the passage. The importance of understanding the passage cannot be overemphasized. So, your first job in answering a Strengthen question is to carefully read the passage, developing understanding of what the passage says and noting key details of the passage.

item.title: 4.5 Common Ways to Strengthen an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content While there are many ways in which an argument can be strengthened, the following five ways show up consistently in GMAT Strengthen the Argument questions: Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion Explicitly State an Assumption upon which the Argument Depends Address an Issue that Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion Rule out an Alternate Cause of an Effect Confirm a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable Let’s discuss each of these.

item.title: 4.6 Provide a Premise to Support or Add Support for the Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. 💡MUST KNOW: In some Strengthen the Argument questions, the passages contain conclusions without any supporting premises. In other questions, the premises within the arguments only partially support the conclusions. In any of these cases, an effective way for an answer choice to strengthen the argument is to provide a premise, which will either provide the only support for the conclusion or provide further support for the conclusion alongside existing premises.

item.title: 4.7 Explicitly State an Assumption Upon Which the Argument Depends
item.description: Lesson Content As you well know, an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true in order for the logic of the argument to hold. Every argument relies upon many assumptions; it isn’t possible to include every single piece of relevant information in an argument. When an assumption is uncontroversial (it is something that “everyone would agree is true”), the presence in the argument of that unstated assumption has no impact on the strength of the argument.

item.title: 4.8 Address an Issue That Could Affect the Validity of the Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content Often, in a scenario described in a Strengthen the Argument passage, there is room for issues that could undermine the conclusion. For example, imagine if the CEO of an electric car company made the claim that his electric cars are much more environmentally friendly than their gas-powered counterparts because, while gas-powered cars emit greenhouse gases, electric cars do not. In making that argument, he makes many assumptions, one of which is that there is no part of the electric car production process that could cause enough harm to the environment that his argument would be unsound.

item.title: 4.9 Rule Out an Alternate Cause of an Effect
item.description: Lesson Content In the arguments found in Strengthen the Argument questions, authors will often use cause-and-effect reasoning; in other words, they will link an effect to a cause. 💡MUST KNOW: Whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that there cannot be an alternative explanation for the effect observed. In other words, he assumes that his cause-and-effect claim is completely accurate. In other words, whenever we can rule out an alternative explanation for an observed effect, we have strengthened the argument.

item.title: 4.10 Confirming a Conclusion by Changing, Adding, or Eliminating a Variable
item.description: Lesson Content You are probably familiar with how many scientific experiments work: a hypothesis is presented, and then experiments are done in which variables are changed to see how changes in variables affect results. For instance, a researcher who feels that children’s eating sugar makes learning difficult for them could confirm that hypothesis by having a control group and an experimental group. If the children in the control group were to learn more easily than the children in the experimental group, the researcher could say that the results support the hypothesis that sugar consumption was causing the children in the experimental group to have difficulty learning.

item.title: 4.11 The Difference Between Trap Choices and Correct Answers in Strengthen Questions
item.description: Lesson Content In most Strengthen the Argument questions, along with the correct answer, there are one or more answer choices that can appear to strengthen the argument while not actually doing so. Noticing the difference between these so called “trap” choices and the correct answer is generally the key final move in choosing the correct answer to a Strengthen the Argument question. Trap Choice: A trap choice in a Strengthen the Argument question says something that is somehow related to the argument and that seems to strengthen the argument, and at the same time, what the trap choice says is not logically connected to the argument in a way such that it strengthens the argument.

item.title: 4.12 Common Incorrect Choices in Strengthen Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Over time, the types of common incorrect choices that appear in Strengthen questions have been predictable. Let’s begin by discussing incorrect choices that do not affect the strength of arguments in any way.

item.title: 4.13 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 1: Provides Information that Does Not Affect the Argument in any Way
item.description: Lesson Content The most blatantly wrong answer choices in Strengthen questions simply do not affect the arguments. In many cases, such choices are fairly easy to eliminate. Consider the following example: The government of Golado has recently increased the taxes imposed on the sale of all alcoholic beverages in Golado. While the tax increase is sure to result in a significant decrease in consumption of alcohol in Golado, government officials believe that increasing the taxes on sales of alcohol will result in a reduction in Golado’s government budget gap.

item.title: 4.14 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 2: Information That Seems to Confirm the Validity of a Premise
item.description: Lesson Content In Strengthen questions, there can be choices that seem to strengthen arguments by providing information that seems to confirm the validity of premises. 💡MUST KNOW: Some incorrect answers to Strengthen questions are incorrect because their only function is to seem to confirm the validity of premises. Since the premises are already considered facts, these answer choices do not affect the strength of arguments. Consider the following example: Over the past decade, most people in Ostazia have significantly increased their consumption of fresh fruit.

item.title: 4.15 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 3: Choice That Supports the Wrong Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content As we know, to arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. 💡MUST KNOW: To arrive at the correct answer to a Strengthen question, we have to clearly understand exactly what conclusion we are seeking to support. If we don’t, we might fall for an answer choice that supports a conclusion that is similar to the conclusion we are seeking to support but is not that conclusion.

item.title: 4.16 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 4: Explains a Part of the Stimulus
item.description: Lesson Content We must always remember that the premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. 💡MUST KNOW: The premises in Critical Reasoning arguments are considered factual. In other words, the premises are facts. Since there is no way to strengthen a fact, there is no way to strengthen a premise. Therefore, it’s important to know that answer choices that explain part of a passage do not strengthen an argument because, as we just said, premises are facts, and even if we were to provide a 100-page essay explaining a fact, we couldn’t strengthen it.

item.title: 4.17 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 5: Information not Well Connected to the Scenario in the Argument
item.description: Lesson Content A typical argument in a Strengthen question is based on a scenario that involves, for example, a particular place, company, phenomenon, or point in time. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering a Strengthen question, we must take care not to select as correct choices that aren’t well-connected to the scenario in the argument. So, what does it mean to be “not well-connected?” The truth of the matter is that what the GMAT considers not well-connected might be different from what we’d expect (sorry – don’t kill the messenger).

item.title: 4.18 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 6: Pseudo-Strengthener
item.description: Lesson Content Some answers to Strengthen questions are subtly written in a way such that they seem to strengthen the arguments, and, at the same time, they don’t really get the job done. 💡MUST KNOW: Some answers to Strengthen questions are subtly written in a way such that they seem to strengthen the arguments, and, at the same time, they don’t really get the job done. So, we could call them “pseudo-strengtheners.

item.title: 4.19 Strengthen Incorrect Choice Type 7: Weakens the Argument
item.description: Lesson Content If the goal in answering a Strengthen question is to find an answer choice that strengthens the argument, then clearly a choice that weakens the argument must be incorrect. However, when a test-taker is under the pressure of the test, answers that weaken rather than strengthen the arguments in Strengthen questions can be tempting because they are closely related to the given information and, thus, can seem to provide just the type of information needed to answer the question.

item.title: 4.20 Other Tricky Question Stems: “EXCEPT,” “NOT,” “LEAST”
item.description: Lesson Content Strengthen the Argument questions will sometimes include words such as “except,” “not,” or “least” in their question stems to make the questions a bit more challenging. Each of the following, if true, provides additional support for the above conclusion EXCEPT: If all of the following are true, which of them does not provide further justification for the contention made? Assuming that all of the following statements are true, which does the LEAST to strengthen the argument?

item.title: 5.1 Introduction to Resolve the Paradox Questions
item.description: Lesson Content A Resolve the Paradox question is based on a passage that presents an apparent paradox, which, in GMAT CR, is a situation in which there exist two facts that seemingly contradict each other or that seemingly cannot both be true at the same time. In other words, a paradox is composed of two seemingly incompatible statements of fact; the facts, at least without more information, don’t seem to make sense together.

item.title: 5.2 Common Resolve the Paradox Question Stems
item.description: Lesson Content The faster we can recognize what a Critical Reasoning question is asking us to find, the faster we can answer it. Here are some of the most common question stems you’ll see: Which of the following, if true, most helps to resolve the apparent discrepancy in the passage above? Which of the following, if true, would explain the discrepancy described above? Which one of the following, if true, does most to justify this apparently paradoxical belief?

item.title: 5.3 Important Resolve the Paradox Keywords
item.description: Lesson Content In Resolve the Paradox Questions, the two seemingly contradictory statements will generally be connected by a keyword that indicates a contrast. yet, but, paradoxically, however, despite, unlike, even though, though, although, instead, rather, surprisingly, unexpectedly 💡MUST KNOW: Learn to recognize the keywords that indicate a contrast between the two seemingly contradictory facts. In Paradox questions, contrast keywords often appear between the two paradoxical facts. Notice the use of the contrast word in the example below.

item.title: 5.4 Strategy for Answering Resolve the Paradox Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To answer Resolve the Paradox questions correctly, follow this 5-step strategy. Step 1 – Carefully read and completely understand the passage. As is the case in answering any other type of Critical Reasoning question, the foundation of choosing the right answer to a Paradox question is understanding the passage. Step 2 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do. The nature of the information presented in the passage, along with the question stem, should make it easy to tell that you’re faced with a Paradox question.

item.title: 5.5 In answering a Paradox question, we must understand exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.
item.description: Lesson Content Precision in reading is one of the most important Critical Reasoning skills to master. 💡MUST KNOW: In Resolve the Paradox questions, every word matters. Accordingly, when test-takers get Paradox questions incorrect, they often do so because, when reading the passage of a Paradox question, they do not develop a precise understanding of what the paradox is that they need to resolve. In answering a Paradox question, we must have a precise understanding of exactly what the paradox is that we must resolve.

item.title: 5.6 Don’t Look for a Conclusion in a Resolve the Paradox Question.
item.description: Lesson Content As we answer a Paradox question, there is no need to look for a conclusion either in the stimulus or in the correct answer, because the author is not making an argument in the way in which he or she does in a Strengthen or Weaken question. 💡MUST KNOW: We don’t need to find a conclusion to correctly answer a Resolve the Paradox question. So, don’t look for one!

item.title: 5.7 In Answering Resolve the Paradox Questions, Look for What Could be True, Not What Must be True.
item.description: Lesson Content For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox. 💡MUST KNOW: For any Resolve the Paradox question, there will likely be many possible ways in which we could resolve the paradox. Our job is just to find one of them. We’re not trying to find the one answer that must be true. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering a Resolve the Paradox question, we’re looking for an answer that could be true.

item.title: 5.8 Common Resolve the Paradox Incorrect Choices
item.description: Lesson Content The more skilled we are at recognizing incorrect choices in Resolve the Paradox questions, the better we’ll be at working through these questions efficiently and accurately, and the less likely we’ll be to fall for trap choices. Let’s start our discussion of common Resolve the Paradox incorrect choices by reviewing a tricky type of incorrect choice: a choice that explains the wrong thing.

item.title: 5.8.1 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 1: A Choice that Explains the Wrong Thing
item.description: Lesson Content As we have discussed, our job in answering a Resolve the Paradox question is to find a choice that explains how the facts that make up the apparent paradox can coexist. Incorrect choices that explain the wrong thing, in other words, choices that explain something other than how the facts can coexist, are common in Paradox questions. Such a choice can be rather tempting because, even though it doesn’t explain what the correct answer has to explain, it does explain something related to the information presented by the passage.

item.title: 5.8.2 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 2: When the Paradox Involves a Difference Between Two Things but an Answer Choice Shows how the Two are Similar
item.description: Lesson Content We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them. 💡MUST KNOW: We can never use a similarity between two things to explain a paradoxical difference between them. A stimulus may discuss things that are similar in some ways but are different in a way that seems paradoxical. Now, one good strategy for approaching a question involving such a scenario is to look for a choice that explains how the two seemingly similar things are actually different.

item.title: 5.8.3 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 3: A Choice that Makes the Situation More Paradoxical
item.description: Lesson Content Be on the lookout for choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical. 💡MUST KNOW: Beware of choices that actually make the situation described even more paradoxical, that deepen the paradox. A situation can be made more paradoxical by information making the two facts appear to be more contradictory or by the addition of what seems to be more conflict to the scenario presented. Remember, our job is to resolve the paradox, not to add to it.

item.title: 5.8.4 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 4: A Choice that Seems to Contradict One of the Two Facts
item.description: Lesson Content In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, our job is not to call into question the credibility of any statement, nor is it to reinforce the credibility of any statement. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering Resolve the Paradox questions, we accept the statements in the passages as true, even though they apparently conflict. Accordingly, we never resolve the paradox in a Paradox question by challenging one of the facts stated in the passage.

item.title: 5.8.5 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 5: A Choice That Introduces Information That Has No Effect On The Paradox
item.description: Lesson Content Among the answer choices in a Paradox question can be choices that have no effect on the paradox presented by the stimulus. 💡MUST KNOW: Information that has no effect on the paradox will never resolve the paradox. To tell the difference between incorrect choices that have no effect on the paradox and the correct answer, we have to carefully consider what effect each choice has on the scenario presented in the stimulus and be careful not to make up convoluted stories that give answer choices effects that they don’t really have.

item.title: 5.8.6 Paradox Incorrect Choice Type 6: Discusses the Facts that Make up the Apparent Paradox Without Explaining How they can Coexist
item.description: Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest type of incorrect choice that can appear in a Resolve the Paradox question is one that discusses the facts or aspects of the facts that make up the apparent paradox but does not resolve the paradox. Consider the following example: In order to spur scientific research, the government of Golado instituted a new program that involved giving significant financial awards and recognition to researchers who made breakthrough discoveries.

item.title: 6.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content A cause-and-effect relationship is a relationship in which one event, X, causes another event, Y, to occur. 💡MUST KNOW: A cause-and-effect relationship is one in which one event causes another event to occur. Many arguments found in Critical Reasoning questions involve cause-and-effect relationships, or relationships that appear to be cause-and-effect relationships. In fact, you’ve already encountered arguments of this type several times in the preceding chapters. Let’s begin this chapter by reviewing, and elaborating on, the assumptions that are inherent to any cause-and-effect argument.

item.title: 6.2 The Assumptions Inherent to All Cause-and-Effect Arguments
item.description: Lesson Content As we learned in prior chapters, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events. In other words, when an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she posits that the cause she has put forth is the ONLY possible cause. 💡MUST KNOW: When an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, she assumes that her cause-and-effect claim is actually correct; she assumes that there could not be an alternative explanation for the observed events.

item.title: 6.3 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning
item.description: Lesson Content Humans tend to seek to see patterns and relationships in situations. 💡MUST KNOW: Humans often make incorrect assumptions about cause-and-effect relationships. For example, there’s a story of an old woman who lived in a tiny village. The old woman made a classic mistake in cause-and-effect reasoning. Causes and effects are very difficult to assign. 💡MUST KNOW: Causes and effects are very difficult to assign. So, we must be very careful any time we encounter a question dealing with cause and effect.

item.title: 6.3.1 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning Error One: Sequencing
item.description: Lesson Content Mistakes involving sequencing happen when we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y. 💡MUST KNOW: We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time we notice that one event, X, occurs before another event, Y, and, on this basis alone, we conclude that the first event, X, must have caused the second, Y.

item.title: 6.3.2 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning Error Two: Co-Occurrence
item.description: Lesson Content A second mistake in reasoning happens when two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on this basis alone, that they are causally linked. 💡MUST KNOW: We’re apt to make an error in reasoning any time two or more events occur simultaneously, and we conclude, on the basis of their simultaneous occurrence alone, that they are causally linked. For example, consider two events, X and Y, that occurred at the same time.

item.title: 6.3.3 Cause-and-effect Reasoning Error Three: Confusing Correlation and Causation
item.description: Lesson Content We say that two variables are correlated when a change in one variable accompanies a change in the other. 💡MUST KNOW: Two variables are correlated when the variables change together in a consistent manner. In a positive correlation, as one variable goes up (or down), so does the other. In a negative correlation, as one variable goes up (or down) the other does the opposite. Now, the fact that two variables are correlated (i.

item.title: 6.4 Weakening A Cause-and-Effect Argument
item.description: Lesson Content As you already know, we tend to see cause-and-effect arguments appear in Weaken the Argument questions.

item.title: 6.4.1 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Reversing the Relationship
item.description: Lesson Content Let’s say that you were to express a wish to a friend that you want to attain a v-shaped physique. One way to weaken the case for your friend’s conclusion would be to show that perhaps the cause and the effect are in fact reversed. In other words, what if we had information that indicates that it may be that only people who are somehow “naturally” built with v-shaped physiques thrive as professional swimmers?

item.title: 6.4.2 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Finding an Alternative Cause For the Effect
item.description: Lesson Content Often, there can be an alternative explanation for an event. 💡MUST KNOW: One way to weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that there is an alternative explanation for the observed effect. Consider the following argument: In the past, almost all personal computer users used the MT operating system. Two years ago, a new operating system called AX was introduced, and, since then, AX has become increasingly popular.

item.title: 6.4.3 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Finding a Third Variable That Causes Both of the Observed Events
item.description: Lesson Content Another way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to find a third variable that causes both of the observed events. 💡MUST KNOW: We can weaken a cause-and-effect argument by showing that there is a common cause for the two variables in question, rather than a direct cause-and-effect link between the two variables in question. For example, let’s say that a statistician notices that, during the winter months, hospital admissions for frostbite increase, as does the number of people wearing coats.

item.title: 6.4.4 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Effect Does Not Occur When the Supposed Cause Occurs
item.description: Lesson Content In some cases, the correct answer to a Weaken question involving a cause-and-effect argument will weaken the support for the conclusion by showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring. 💡MUST KNOW: When dealing with a cause-and-effect argument, showing that the supposed cause occurs without the effect occurring will weaken the argument. After all, if there is a cause-and-effect relationship at play, when we see the cause, we should also see the effect.

item.title: 6.4.5 Weakening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Effect Occurs Even When the Supposed Cause Does Not Occur
item.description: Lesson Content Just as we can weaken the support for a conclusion about a cause-and-effect relationship by showing that the supposed cause happens without the effect’s happening, we can also weaken the support for such a conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not. 💡MUST KNOW: We can weaken the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion by showing that the effect happens even when the supposed cause does not.

item.title: 6.5 Strengthening the Support for a Cause-and-Effect Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content As we saw in the Strengthen the Argument chapter, there are many ways to strengthen an argument. Ruling out alternative causes. Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur. Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed. 💡MUST KNOW: Three ways that are commonly used to strengthen the support for cause-and-effect conclusions are the following: 1) Ruling out alternative causes, 2) Showing that when the cause is not present the effect does not occur, 3) Showing that the relationship cannot be reversed.

item.title: 6.5.1 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Ruling Out Alternative Causes
item.description: Lesson Content Since one way to weaken a cause-and-effect argument is to present an alternate cause for the observed effect, one way to strengthen a cause-and-effect argument is to rule out an alternative cause for the effect. 💡MUST KNOW: One way to strengthen the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion is to rule out alternative causes. Remember that, whenever an author makes a cause-and-effect claim, he assumes that his claim is accurate.

item.title: 6.5.2 Scientifically Designed Experiments
item.description: Lesson Content The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable. 💡MUST KNOW: The major goal of an experiment is to isolate a single variable and measure its effect on another variable. In scientific studies, the method most commonly used for ruling out alternative causes is to have a control group alongside the true test group, which is also referred to as the experimental group.

item.title: 6.5.3 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That, When the Cause is Not Present, the Effect Does Not Occur
item.description: Lesson Content In the question above that involved the diets of the two groups of Timas, the central way in which the correct answer strengthened the argument was to show what happens when the diet of heavily processed foods is not present. Showing that, when the cause is not present, the effect does not occur is one of the ways in which correct answers to Strengthen questions commonly strengthen support for conclusions about cause-and-effect relationships.

item.title: 6.5.4 Strengthening a Cause-and-Effect Argument by Showing That the Relationship Cannot Be Reversed
item.description: Lesson Content Remember that, when an argument claims that A is the cause of B, we can weaken that argument by showing that it’s likely that B causes A. Therefore, another way in which we can strengthen the conclusion in a cause-and-effect argument is to show that the relationship cannot be reversed. 💡MUST KNOW: One way to strengthen a cause-and-effect conclusion is to show that the relationship between the cause and the effect cannot be reversed.

item.title: 6.6 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning in Resolve the Paradox Questions
item.description: Lesson Content When a cause-and-effect scenario appears in a Paradox question, we won’t have to find a choice that affects the support for a cause-and-effect conclusion, since Paradox questions don’t focus on support for conclusions. Consider the following example: In Australia, a fence was built to keep dingoes out of areas in which ranchers have livestock. It was expected that small marsupials, upon which dingoes prey, would thrive in the areas without dingoes, but instead the numbers of small marsupials in areas without dingoes are much lower than the numbers of such marsupials in areas where there are still dingoes.

item.title: 6.7 Cause-and-Effect Reasoning in Other Types of Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Now that we have extensively discussed cause-and-effect reasoning and how such reasoning can appear in various types of Critical Reasoning questions, let’s continue with our exploration of GMAT Critical Reasoning.

item.title: 7.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts. 💡MUST KNOW: An inference is a conclusion that can be drawn from a set of facts. On the GMAT, a Critical Reasoning Inference question is a question that presents a set of facts and then asks which of the answer choices states a conclusion that can be properly inferred from that set of facts.

item.title: 7.2 Basic Guidelines for Correctly Answering Inference Questions
item.description: Lesson Content There are a number of smart strategies that we can use when answering Inference questions. Never Bring in Outside Information or Knowledge We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimuli; we must not bring in any outside information. 💡MUST KNOW: We must answer Inference questions using only the facts provided to us in the stimulus; we must not bring in any outside information.

item.title: 7.3 Recognizing Inference Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Every Inference question on the GMAT begins with a passage or other stimulus, which is followed by a question that asks, in one way or another, which of the answer choices states something that must be true given what is said in the stimulus. While Inference questions can be worded in various ways, GMAT Critical Reasoning questions tend to utilize predictable language, a fact that we can exploit by familiarizing ourselves with the types of questions we will see.

item.title: 7.4 Inference Question Strategy
item.description: Lesson Content Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage As is the case in answering any other type of Critical Reasoning question, the foundation of choosing the right answer to an Inference question is understanding the passage. Also, remember that, when answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said. 💡MUST KNOW: When answering an Inference question, misreading a single word in the stimulus can significantly change the implications of what is said.

item.title: 7.5 Common Inference Question Trap Answer Types
item.description: Lesson Content While we know that there is only one correct answer to each Inference question, in most Inference questions, as we have already seen, some of the incorrect answers may somehow rather convincingly seem to be correct and, thus, may tempt test takers for a variety of reasons. In order to mentally prepare you to handle these trap answers, and thus reduce the likelihood of your falling for them, let’s go over some of the types of trap answers that can appear in Inference questions.

item.title: 7.5.1 An Answer That Could be True or is Likely to be True
item.description: Lesson Content Remember that an Inference question requires us to find an answer that must be true. If we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering an Inference question, if we select an answer that only could be true or is only likely to be true, we are by definition selecting an incorrect answer.

item.title: 7.5.2 An Answer that Presents Information from the Stimulus in a Distorted Fashion
item.description: Lesson Content Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read. 💡MUST KNOW: Unless we are paying very close attention when answering a Critical Reasoning question, it’s easy to forget and or twist some of what we read. Thus, we may create a distorted picture of what we read. Such a distorted picture makes it a lot easier for a test-taker to fall for trap answers that include unsupported information.

item.title: 7.5.3 An Answer that is out of Proportion to the Information that is Presented in the Stimulus
item.description: Lesson Content An “out of proportion” answer is one that exaggerates information presented in the stimulus. For example, if a passage were to suggest that “some people in Group A like basketball,” while an answer choice implies that “all people in Group A are basketball fans,” the choice would be out of proportion and incorrect. Let’s say there are ten people in Group A, then “some” could be three people, four people, or even ten people.

item.title: 7.5.4 An Answer That Seems to Be Supported by the Stimulus but Actually Depends on Other Information
item.description: Lesson Content In answering Inference questions, we must be extremely careful with answer choices that bring in new information. 💡MUST KNOW: In an Inference question, an answer choice that depends on support from information beyond what’s presented in the stimulus can’t be correct. Consider the following example: While surgery to treat cataracts restores vision clarity quickly, it is somewhat expensive. At the same time, there are relatively inexpensive holistic methods that some people who experience cataracts could use to clear their vision within a reasonable timeframe, just as effectively though not as quickly as surgery does.

item.title: 7.5.5 An Answer that is True in the Real World but is not Supported by the Stimulus
item.description: Lesson Content In reading Inference questions, you may encounter incorrect answer choices that say things that are true in the real world but are not completely supported by the information presented to us in the stimulus. Consider the following example: The cost of solar panels that can be used by homeowners to generate electricity has been decreasing. Over the past seven years, the cost per watt of power generation capacity has decreased by 50 percent, and this trend is expected to continue.

item.title: 7.6 Correct Answer Types in Inference Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Just as knowing how incorrect answers may work can be helpful, a solid understanding of how correct answers may look can help us to efficiently and correctly answer Inference questions.

item.title: 7.6.1 Answers that are Conclusions Based on the Entirety of What is Said in the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content The first type of Inference correct answer that we’ll discuss is an answer that is a conclusion based on the entirety of the information presented in the passage. Often, what is conveyed by the statements in the passage in an Inference question adds up logically to a conclusion. Consider the following example: The presence on Mars of certain clay minerals believed to form only in water gave scientists reason to believe that life may have once existed on Mars, in the water in which the clay minerals were formed.

item.title: 7.6.2 Answers that are Fully Supported by Small Portions of the Passages
item.description: Lesson Content Another type of correct answer that often appears in Inference questions is an answer that is fully supported by a small portion of the stimulus. 💡MUST KNOW: In some cases, the correct answers to Inference questions are fully supported by small portions of or even single sentences of the passages. Understand that a correct answer to an Inference question need not be supported by the entire stimulus and that an answer will be correct as long as some portion of the stimulus fully supports the answer.

item.title: 7.6.3 Answers that are Supported by Numerical Information Located Throughout Passages
item.description: Lesson Content A third type of correct answer that we may encounter in an Inference question is an answer that is supported by numerical information located throughout the passage. Finding the correct answer to such a question may require understanding the implications of a ratio or deciphering the meaning of data presented in percentage terms. Consider the following example: Roberta’s Chocolate has always used two ounces of cocoa in making each of its chocolate bars.

item.title: 7.6.4 Answers that are Supported by Mathematical Information Located in Small Portions of Passages
item.description: Lesson Content One variation that can make an Inference question a little trickier is a question’s having a correct answer that is based on some but not all the mathematical information present in a passage. 💡MUST KNOW: When looking for the correct answer to an Inference question involving numerical information, be sure to be open to choosing any choice, not just the ones that seem to be based on all the information presented by the passage.

item.title: 7.7 “Except” Inference Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As is the case with other types of Critical Reasoning questions, the test-makers can increase the difficulty of Inference questions by using the word “EXCEPT” or “not” in question stems. If the statements above are true, all of the following can be properly inferred from them EXCEPT: or Given that the statements above are all true, which of the following is not an inference that can be properly made on the basis of them?

item.title: 8.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Many of the Critical Reasoning questions that we’ve examined up to this point have introduced arguments that contain clearly-stated conclusions. Both names are fitting since these questions ask us to find the author’s logical conclusion or main point. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering Find the Conclusion/Main Point questions, our job is to select an answer choice that best captures the author’s conclusion, the main point of the argument. Consider the following simple example:

item.title: 8.1.1 Finding the Conclusion of an Argument
item.description: Lesson Content Find the Conclusion questions will be based on passages that always contain premises (evidence), and, from time to time, include background information or an intermediate conclusion. 💡MUST KNOW: An argument’s conclusion must be closely connected to its premises, assumptions, and any intermediary conclusions that may be present. It’s important to keep in mind that a conclusion must follow from only the information presented in the argument and any assumptions made by the argument.

item.title: 8.2 Spotting a Find the Conclusion Question
item.description: Lesson Content The question stem for a Find the Conclusion question will ask us to find the best conclusion to an argument among the five possible answer choices. Which of the following conclusions is best supported by the information above? The most reasonable conclusion based on the premises above is that If the statements above are all true, they most strongly support which of the following conclusions? The information above provides the most support for which of the following hypotheses?

item.title: 8.3 The Five-Step Strategy for Answering Find the Conclusion Questions
