item.description: Lesson Content As there are for answering Critical Reasoning questions of other types, there are best practices that we can follow when answering Find the Conclusion questions. Step 1 – Carefully Read and Completely Understand the Passage The foundation of choosing the right answer to a Conclusion question is understanding the passage. Step 2 – Identify the Question Type (What type of information is the question asking for?) Read the question stem to determine what role the correct answer to the question must play.

item.title: 8.3.1 Be Careful not to Confuse Find the Conclusion Questions with Similar-Sounding Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As you’ve seen, other question types may also use words like “concludes” or “conclusion” in their question stems. For example, the question stem “Which of the following best supports the conclusion above?” includes the word “conclusion,” but it’s actually a Strengthen the Argument question stem. 💡MUST KNOW: When reading a Critical Reasoning question stem, we must take the time necessary in order to be sure we’ve correctly identified what the question is asking us to do.

item.title: 8.3.2 The Differences Between Inference Questions and Find the Conclusion Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As you know, inferences are conclusions that must be true if the statements made are true (and the statements in passages are always considered true in Critical Reasoning questions). One major difference between these two types of questions is the solidity of the support that the statements in the stimuli provide for the correct answers to the questions. In an Inference question, the correct answer must be true given what the statements say.

item.title: 8.4 Incorrect Answers in Find the Conclusion Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As we have been discussing how to choose the correct answers to various types of Critical Reasoning questions, we have discussed the importance of not making up unsupported, convoluted stories in order to connect answer choices to passages. Find the Conclusion questions turn the process around, by including incorrect answer choices that are unsupported stories that are related to what the premises say in convoluted ways. To make you better prepared to tell the difference between unsupported stories and supported conclusions, let’s now go over some reasons why incorrect answer choices that appear in Find the Conclusion questions are not supported by the stimuli.

item.title: 8.4.1 Wrong Answer Type One: A Choice that Requires the Support of Information that is not Included in the Stimulus
item.description: Lesson Content In Critical Reasoning questions of some types, such as Weaken the Argument questions or Strengthen the Argument questions, the correct answer will contain new information that is not found in the passage. However, in a Find the Conclusion question, all of the information needed to answer the question must already be included in the passage. 💡MUST KNOW: In a Find the Conclusion question, an answer that depends on outside information will be incorrect.

item.title: 8.4.2 Wrong Answer Type Two: A Choice that Extends Beyond what the Statements in the Stimulus Actually Say, i.e., Goes Too Far
item.description: Lesson Content As we’ve discussed in past chapters, when we read information, we may put our own spin on the statements that we are reading. For instance, a passage might support the conclusion that a company “will become more profitable,” and an incorrect answer choice will say that the company “will become the most profitable company in its sector.” In this case, the incorrect answer choice goes too far when it says that the company will be the “most profitable,” as the passage supports only the conclusion that the company will become “more profitable.

item.title: 8.4.3 Wrong Answer Type Three: An Answer that Is Related to What the Premises Say yet Is Not Actually Supported by Them
item.description: Lesson Content A conclusion is a synthesis of the premises of an argument. The creators of GMAT Critical Reasoning questions realize that, in the heat of taking the test, a test-taker can be tempted to take a shortcut and select a choice that includes subject matter similar to that of the passage without carefully comparing the implications of the statements in the passage with the conclusion stated in the choice.

item.title: 8.4.4 Wrong Answer Type Four: An Answer that Conflicts in Some Way with Information that Appears in the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content One aspect of the GMAT is that it tests a test-taker’s degree of attention to detail, and one way in which the GMAT tests attention to detail is by including in a Find the Conclusion question choices that may seem to follow from what the passage says but actually conflict with some detail of what the passage says. Consider the following example: The city of Danterton gets tax revenue by adding a sales tax based on a percentage of the price of items sold to the sale of all retail merchandise sold in Danterton.

item.title: 9.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Evaluate the Argument questions present a plan, a proposal, or an argument and then ask us to find an answer that highlights information that helps us to evaluate whether the plan or proposal will work or whether the conclusion of the argument is valid. 💡MUST KNOW: Evaluate the Argument questions present a plan, a proposal, or an argument and then ask us to find an answer that highlights information that helps us to evaluate whether the plan or proposal will work or whether the conclusion of the argument is valid.

item.title: 9.2 The Yes/No Test for Evaluate the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument questions can be solved via the use of what’s called the Yes/No test. In many cases, the answer choices in Evaluate the Argument questions pose questions that can be answered in two ways, either “Yes” or “No.” In such a case, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument Question must be a question such that, if it is answered “Yes”, the argument is affected in one way (either strengthened or weakened), and, if it is answered “No,” the argument is affected in the reverse way.

item.title: 9.3 Common Question Stems for Evaluate the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Evaluate the Argument questions use predictable language, such as: In evaluating the argument, it would be most useful to establish which of the following? Which of the following pieces of information would be most useful in evaluating the logic of the argument presented above? Which of the following would be most useful in determining whether the conclusion is valid? To evaluate the strength of the argument, it would be most helpful to know which of the following?

item.title: 9.4 Strategy for Attacking Evaluate the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To solve Evaluate the Argument Questions, follow this strategy: Step 1 – Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument In an Evaluate the Argument question, the passage will present a plan, proposal, or argument. So, your first job in answering an Evaluate the Argument question is to carefully and methodically read the passage to determine what the point of the passage is and how any argument made in the passage is supported.

item.title: 9.5 Ways to Handle the Four Types of Answer Choices That Appear in Evaluate the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content In Evaluate the Argument questions, there are four forms in which answer choices can appear: Questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No” Questions that can be answered in two ways that are not “Yes” or “No” Questions of other types Statements, rather than questions We have already discussed how to handle answer choices that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” We use the Yes/No test and consider whether the “Yes” and “No” answers strengthen or weaken the arguments.

item.title: 9.5.1 Answer Choices That Pose Questions That Can Be Answered in Two Ways That Are Not “Yes” or “No”
item.description: Lesson Content Many Evaluate the Argument answer choices allow us to apply the Yes/No test, because the choices pose questions that can be answered “Yes” or “No.” However, other answer choices can be structured in ways such that the Yes/No test doesn’t work if we apply the test literally. Consider the following question: What proportion of lower income New York City residents travel to Manhattan by subway rather than by car?

item.title: 9.5.2 Answer Choices that Pose Questions of Other Types
item.description: Lesson Content In most cases, Evaluate the Argument answer choices will pose questions that can be either answered “Yes” and “No” or answered with two extreme answers. For example: Why did the divorce rate decrease? What other types of plants are attacked by the fungus? There is not one particular way in which to handle all such answer choices. Does the reason for the divorce rate decrease affect the support for the argument?

item.title: 9.5.3 Answer Choices that are Statements rather than Questions
item.description: Lesson Content So far, in all of the Evaluate the Argument questions that we have considered, the answer choices have been in the form of questions. All the same, to answer Evaluate questions of this type, we can use a method that is similar to the methods that we have been using. Consider the following example: In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport.

item.title: 9.6 Trap Choices in Evaluate the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As we have discussed, the correct answer to an Evaluate the Argument question is a question or statement that highlights information that we could use to weaken or strengthen the argument. In writing Evaluate the Argument questions, question writers come up with all kinds of ways to create trap choices that seem to highlight information that affects the arguments but don’t really do so. carefully note the conclusion the validity of which is being evaluated.

item.title: 9.7 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 1: Highlights Information that is Pertinent to the Topic that the Argument is About but Doesn’t Affect the Strength of the Argument
item.description: Lesson Content There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic. 💡MUST KNOW: There is a difference between information that is pertinent to a topic and information that affects the support for a specific conclusion about that topic. For instance, in a general discussion of a problem, information on how to solve the problem would be pertinent.

item.title: 9.8 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 2: Brings Up an Irrelevant Comparison
item.description: Lesson Content Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments. 💡MUST KNOW: Comparisons can often be used to strengthen or weaken support for conclusions and, thus, can be helpful for evaluating arguments. Of course, only a relevant comparison will be useful in this way, and the incorrect answer choices to Evaluate questions often highlight comparisons that indicate nothing that is useful for evaluating the arguments.

item.title: 9.9 Evaluate Incorrect Choice Type 3: Highlights Information that Misses the Point and Affects the Wrong Conclusion
item.description: Lesson Content In reviewing Weaken the Argument questions and Strengthen the Argument questions, we saw that some answer choices affect conclusions that are related to the conclusions of the arguments but are not the conclusions that we need to affect. For example, we could have an argument the conclusion of which is that, by educating the public, avant garde artists could increase the market for their work. Consider the following example:

item.title: 10.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content In our review of GMAT Critical Reasoning questions, we have seen many arguments that are flawed. Logical Flaw questions also present us with stimuli containing flawed arguments, but the focus of Logical Flaw questions is somewhat different from the foci of the other types of questions, in that Logical Flaw questions are more directly focused on the flaws in the arguments. choose an answer that best describes a flaw found in the argument (in the case of a pure Logical Flaw question).

item.title: 10.1.1 The Connection Between Assumptions and Logical Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content Remember – an assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the logic of an argument to be sound. 💡MUST KNOW: An assumption is a piece of missing information that must be true for the support for an argument to be sound. As you know well by this point, authors make assumptions when making arguments. Let’s look at a few examples: Health insurance for those living in major cities costs significantly more than does health insurance for those living in small towns.

item.title: 10.2 Common Logical Flaw Question Stems
item.description: Lesson Content As you already know, Critical Reasoning question stems are predictable, and the more quickly we can determine what we have to do in order to correctly answer the question we’re facing, the more time we’ll have for answering it. The following are common ways in which Logical Flaw Questions are worded: Which of the following best describes the error of reasoning contained in the argument above? Which of the following best describes a weakness present in the argument above?

item.title: 10.2.1 Pure Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The more basic type of Logical Flaw question is a pure Logical Flaw question. 💡MUST KNOW: Some Logical Flaw Questions are pure Logical Flaw questions, meaning that they ask us to point out a flaw in the argument as it currently stands and without bringing in new information in the correct answer. Let’s look at an example stimulus of a pure Logical Flaw question: In a recent medical study, all of the study participants who had in the past five years abused barbiturates, powerful prescription sedatives commonly used to treat anxiety, were found to be suffering with depression.

item.title: 10.2.2 Reviewing the Difference Between a Logical Flaw Question and a Weaken the Argument Question
item.description: Lesson Content Logical Flaw questions may seem to be rather similar to Weaken the Argument questions. As we’ve discussed, almost always, in order to weaken the conclusion in a Weaken the Argument question, the correct answer option brings in new, outside information. On the other hand, in answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument. 💡MUST KNOW: In answering a pure Logical Flaw question, we are not seeking to weaken the argument.

item.title: 10.2.3 Logical Flaw Questions Hybridized with Weaken the Argument Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The second Logical Flaw question type contains questions that are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. 💡MUST KNOW: Some Logical Flaw Questions are hybrids of Weaken the Argument questions and pure Logical Flaw questions. These questions also ask us to point out a flaw in the author’s argument, but they bring in new information in the correct answer, in a way quite similar to the way in which regular Weaken the Argument questions do.

item.title: 10.2.4 Common Flaws in Reasoning Found in Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content There are several mistakes (flaws) in reasoning that authors commonly make in their arguments. Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect Overgeneralizing and Making Sampling Errors Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages or Other Numerical Information Conclusions that are not Supported by the Evidence Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information The good news is that any of these concepts that are fair game in Weaken the Argument questions are also fair game in Logical Flaw questions.

item.title: 10.2.5 Logical Flaw One: Errors in Reasoning Involving Cause and Effect
item.description: Lesson Content Authors of arguments often misconstrue or misperceive the nature of cause-and-effect relationships. In Logical Flaw questions, we often see authors and speakers making mistakes associated with cause-and-effect reasoning. The most common mistakes center on the following three flaws in reasoning: Here’s an error in cause-and-effect reasoning in the context of a Logical Flaw question: The transportation secretary of the city of Niezque noticed that, in areas of the city in which there were many bicycle repair shops, there tended to be great numbers of people using bicycles.

item.title: 10.2.6 Logical Flaw Two: Overgeneralization and Sampling Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content As we have already discussed in another chapter, when making a generalization, we base a broad claim about a larger population upon observations of a small sample of a population. For example, let’s say that we know ten people who live in Dallas, and all vote Republican. In answering Logical Flaw questions, be on the lookout for broad generalizations made from small samples; such generalizations will be suspect.

item.title: 10.2.7 Logical Flaw Three: Confusion Related to Numbers, Percentages, or Other Numerical Information
item.description: Lesson Content As has been discussed in other chapters, authors commonly make errors in reasoning based on numbers and percentages. Consider the following example: During the past 25 years, the percentage of Greysoft employees who invest their retirement monies in the company mutual fund has decreased by fifteen percent. Since a certain number of investors withdraw their money from the company mutual fund each year, to function optimally, the mutual fund requires a consistent number of new investors.

item.title: 10.2.8 Logical Flaw Four: Conclusions that are Not Supported by the Evidence
item.description: Lesson Content Sometimes an author will draw a conclusion that’s simply not supported by the evidence provided. For example, an author could seek to support the conclusion that a product is safe to use by going on about all the testing of the product that has been done. Notice how the evidence in the following example doesn’t really support the conclusion. Professor: Over the past few years, I have noticed that the students coming to our school are less motivated than students here were in previous years.

item.title: 10.2.9 Logical Flaw Five: Confusing Necessary or Significant Information with Sufficient Information
item.description: Lesson Content Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways. 💡MUST KNOW: Necessary information, significant information, and sufficient information differ from each other in key ways. At the same time, since they are somewhat similar, these types of information can be misused in arguments, in which cases the arguments are flawed. A key thing to understand about these types of information is that information that is necessary or significant is not necessarily the same as information that is sufficient for supporting a conclusion.

item.title: 10.3 Types of Incorrect Answers in Logical Flaw Questions
item.description: Lesson Content A key aspect of choosing correct answers to Logical Flaw questions is, of course, not getting tempted into choosing answer choices that are correct sounding but incorrect. Let’s go through some types of Logical Flaw incorrect answers to better prepare you to see them for what they are.

item.title: 10.3.1 Incorrect Choices that Describe Things that the Arguments Do Not Actually Do
item.description: Lesson Content The most obviously wrong answer choices in Logical Flaw questions are those that describe things that authors or arguments don’t actually do. Consider the following example: Unlike other major automobile companies, all of which produce mostly automobiles that are fossil fuel powered, The Blitz Automobile Company makes only electric automobiles. Because electric automobiles are easier to maintain, less costly to power, and more environmentally friendly, they are superior to fossil fuel powered vehicles.

item.title: 10.3.2 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Things that the Argument Does but are not Flaws
item.description: Lesson Content In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. 💡MUST KNOW: In order for a Logical Flaw answer choice to be correct, it has to have two characteristics. It has to describe something that actually goes on in the argument, and what it describes has to be a flaw in the argument. Many Logical Flaw incorrect answer choices satisfy the first criterion, as they describe things that the authors or arguments actually do, but do not satisfy the second one, as what they describe are not flaws.

item.title: 10.3.3 Incorrect Choices that Describe Flaws in the Support for the Wrong Conclusions
item.description: Lesson Content As we have seen in reviewing various types of Critical Reasoning questions, one way in which the creator of a question can create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument. 💡MUST KNOW: One way to create a tempting wrong answer choice is to have the choice address a conclusion that is closely related to the conclusion of the argument but is not actually the conclusion of the argument.

item.title: 10.3.4 Incorrect Choices that Bring Up Mathematical Information that Doesn’t Matter
item.description: Lesson Content The argument in a Logical Flaw question can have a conclusion that is math related, such as a conclusion based on population data, a conclusion based on research results, or a conclusion about company profits. 💡MUST KNOW: Logical Flaw questions involving mathematical arguments present an opportunity for a question writer to create answer choices that are tempting but incorrect because they bring up mathematical information that doesn’t matter for the support of the conclusion presented.

item.title: 11.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space. 💡MUST KNOW: Complete the Passage questions are among the easiest Critical Reasoning questions to identify, principally because they end with a blank space. When we see a passage with a long, underlined segment at the end, “__________,” we know that we’re dealing with a Complete the Passage Question.

item.title: 11.2 The Skills Used in Answering Complete the Passage Questions are Similar to those Used for Answering Other Types of Critical Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The good news is that, while the format of a Complete the Passage question may seem new, in answering a Complete the Passage question, we won’t actually have to do anything particularly different from what other types of Critical Reasoning questions ask us to do. For example, a Complete the Passage question could ask us to do any one of the following: · State an assumption made by the author

item.title: 11.3 Exposing the Traps in a Complete the Passage Question by Converting the Sentence that Ends with a Blank into a Question Beginning with “Why” or “What”
item.description: Lesson Content Much of what makes Complete the Passage questions tricky is the fact that the answer choice completes a sentence, and so, an incorrect choice that sounds as if it flows well with the sentence, even though it doesn’t effectively complete the passage, can be very tempting. Consider the following example: Which of the following most logically completes the set of facts presented below? In response to residents’ complaints about constant noise made by airplanes’ taking off and landing, the city council of Mergate is considering permanently closing Mergate Airport.

item.title: 11.4 Strategy For Answering Complete the Passage Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Here is a 5-step strategy for handling Complete the Passage questions. Step 1 – Read the question stem and identify what it is asking you to do. In most Complete the Passage questions, the question stems precede the passages. Step 2 – Carefully read, break down, and completely understand the passage. The foundation of choosing the correct answer to a Complete the Passage question is understanding the passage.

item.title: 11.5 The Correct Answer to a Complete the Passage Question Must Work With the Entire Passage, Not With Just the Last Sentence.
item.description: Lesson Content Because a Complete the Passage Question asks us to select an answer option that best completes the passage, it’s easy to focus too much on the last sentence of the stimulus (the one with the blank line in it). Consider the following example: Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired.

item.title: 11.6 The Answer to a Complete the Passage Question Must Work with the Logic of the Passage and not Merely Match the Wording of the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content Trap answers to Complete the Passage questions may utilize words, phrases, and syntax from the passage. We must be vigilant not to choose such wrong answers. Consider the following example: Since the introduction of e-books, many have speculated that traditional “hard copy” book publishing will become largely obsolete in the near future because the book-purchasing public will prefer the more convenient e-books to traditional books. However, this prediction may have been premature, as __________.

item.title: 11.7 Common Incorrect Answer Types for Complete the Passage Questions
item.description: Lesson Content As you already know, Complete the Passage questions are, for the most part, just altered forms of questions of types that you already know well. Therefore, you already have a solid understanding of many of the common types of wrong answers that appear in Complete the Passage questions. At the same time, since Complete the Passage questions have their own unique format, they also have some fairly unique types of incorrect answers.

item.title: 11.7.1 An Answer that Sounds Correct, but Heads in a Subtly Incorrect Direction
item.description: Lesson Content As you know very well at this point, many trap answers in Critical Reasoning questions sound good and appear to be correct, but, at the same time, they are incorrect. In Complete the Passage questions, trap answers are often incorrect because, although they sound correct, they head in directions subtly different from the direction in which the passages were headed. Notice the difference between the answers in the following example:

item.title: 11.7.2 An Answer that States Something that is Plausible or Inferable Given What is Stated in the Passage, but is Not the Logical Completion of the Passage.
item.description: Lesson Content Remember, in answering a Complete the Passage Question, our goal is to find the answer that best completes the passage. Consider the following example: Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled. The machinery that the company uses to remove the shells of walnuts has broken down, and will be replaced with new, more efficient machinery rather than repaired.

item.title: 11.7.3 A Choice That Addresses a Material Issue That is Not, However, the Issue That is the Focus of the Passage.
item.description: Lesson Content When answering a Complete the Passage question that discusses a plan or a course of action, it’s important that we do not select as correct an answer choice that addresses a material issue that is not, however, the issue that is the focus of the passage. In a situation, there can exist multiple issues. Consider the following example: Miracle Walnut Company sells two products – bags of walnuts in their shells, and bags of walnuts unshelled.

item.title: 11.7.4 A Choice that Explains Something Discussed in the Passage but does not Complete the Passage
item.description: Lesson Content We’ll wrap up our discussion of incorrect answers in Complete the Passage questions by discussing a type of choice that can be surprisingly tempting, a choice that explains part of the passage rather than completes the passage. Consider the following example: Which of the following best completes the passage? Overfishing in the waters around Seatown has begun to significantly deplete the populations of fish in them. The government of Seatown has proposed setting up in these waters fish farms for raising fish for human consumption, in hopes that focusing on farming fish rather than on catching wild fish will give wild fish stocks a chance to recover.

item.title: 12.1 Introduction
item.description: Lesson Content Unlike many other types of Critical Reasoning questions, Method of Reasoning questions focus on how an argument is structured, not on the merits of the argument. In other words, when we consider the stimulus of a Method of Reasoning question, we don’t need to focus on whether the argument is sound or convincing or weak. 💡MUST KNOW: When solving a Method of Reasoning question, our job is to focus on how the argument is organized and analyze the argument’s structure.

item.title: 12.1.1 Each Part of the Correct Answer Must Be, Well, Correct.
item.description: Lesson Content It’s obvious that, for an answer choice to be correct, that answer choice must be completely correct. We can leverage this fact to help us solve Method of Reasoning questions. The answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of authors’ arguments. 💡MUST KNOW: Since the answers to Method of Reasoning questions describe the logical structures of arguments, a correct answer must describe exactly how a given argument is structured.

item.title: 12.1.2 Common Question Stems for Method of Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Method of Reasoning questions use predictable language, such as: The argument proceeds by The father responds to his daughter’s criticism by doing which of the following? The exchange above supports the claim that the two parties disagree about Which one of the following most accurately describes the relationship between Jane’s argument and Mark’s argument? The Attorney General uses which of the following techniques in responding to the cheese importer?

item.title: 12.2 Two-Part Method of Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Many Method of Reasoning questions are based on dialogues between two speakers. Each part of the dialogue is considered a separate argument, generally with its own evidence, assumption(s), and conclusion(s). The first speaker’s argument may or may not be sound. On the other hand, the second speaker may respond effectively to the first speaker. The two speakers could believe that they agree (or disagree), when in fact they are talking about different things.

item.title: 12.2.1 The Verbs Used by the Speakers Can Help Us Arrive at Correct Answers
item.description: Lesson Content In answering two-part questions, we must determine what type of response the second speaker makes to the first speaker’s argument. The journalist responds to the editor’s criticism by (A) rejecting the editor’s conclusion while proposing … (B) providing evidence that the editor’s argument has significantly … (C) objecting to the editor’s introducing into the discussion … (D) pointing out a benefit of maintaining the board, which … (E) shifting the discussion from the argument at hand to an attack on …

item.title: 12.3 Strategy for Attacking Method of Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content To solve Method of Reasoning Questions, follow this simple strategy: Step 1 - Carefully Read, Break Down, and Completely Understand the Argument The foundation of choosing the right answer to a Method of Reasoning question is understanding the argument presented in the passage. 💡MUST KNOW: Do whatever you must to ensure that you understand the argument presented in a Method of Reasoning question. Go slowly. Read carefully. Take short notes on what you read.

item.title: 12.4 Incorrect Answers in Method of Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Just as is the case with Critical Reasoning questions of all other types, Method of Reasoning questions can include various types of incorrect answers, some of which are tricky trap answers that almost seem correct.

item.title: 12.4.1 An Answer that Presents New Components
item.description: Lesson Content Remember, Method of Reasoning questions specifically ask us to analyze how a given argument is structured. In other words, our analysis and identification will necessarily stick to the structure and wording of the argument presented. For instance, in a question involving an argument that questions the effectiveness of a plan, an answer choice that might say something about questioning the motives of the planners. Consider the following example:

item.title: 12.4.2 Partially Correct Answers
item.description: Lesson Content Partially correct answer choices are always tempting because they typically begin by accurately, or somewhat accurately, describing part of the argument. Notice how the partially correct answer in the following example could be tempting: It is common knowledge that overconsumption of salt can result in excessively high blood pressure. What is not as well known is that heavy consumption of sugar can have similar effects. Results of a 2010 study indicate that consuming a diet high in sugar can lead to an increase in blood pressure greater than that typically resulting from consumption of salt.

item.title: 12.4.3 Answers that Describe Methods that Arguments Could Use but Don’t Use
item.description: Lesson Content Another tricky type of incorrect answer that you could see in a Method of Reasoning question is one that describes a method that the argument could use but doesn’t use. Consider the following example: Commercial landlords in Mennerton are excited about the completion of the new high speed rail line between Mennerton and Boselton, because they believe that, since the new line will make Mennerton more easily accessible, large businesses that would have located in Boselton will now locate instead in Mennerton, where rents are lower.

item.title: 12.4.4 An Answer Choice That Describes a Method That Resembles What the Argument Does but Is Not What the Argument Does
item.description: Lesson Content Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments. 💡MUST KNOW: Perhaps the trickiest incorrect answers in Method of Reasoning questions are the ones that describe methods that somehow resemble methods used in the arguments although the methods described are not actually the methods used in the arguments.

item.title: 12.5 Boldface Method of Reasoning Questions
item.description: Lesson Content Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type. 💡MUST KNOW: Some Method of Reasoning Questions present us with stimuli that include one or two sections that are in boldface type. In answering such a question, our job is to identify the role that any section in boldfaced type plays in the argument. Thus, in Boldface Method of Reasoning questions, the correct answer is the one that accurately describes the role, or roles, of any sections in boldface.

item.title: 12.5.1 Boldface Method of Reasoning Question Stems
item.description: Lesson Content The question stems that appear in Boldface questions generally ask us to determine which answers accurately describe the bolded sections of the passages. In the argument given, the two boldfaced portions play which of the following roles? In the argument above, the portion in boldface plays which of the following roles? In the ecologist’s reasoning, the two portions in boldface play which of the following roles? Which of the following, if true, would serve the same function in the argument as the statement in boldface?

item.title: 12.5.2 Identifying Conclusions in order to Efficiently and Correctly Answer Boldface Questions
item.description: Lesson Content The stimuli of Boldface questions can be hodgepodges of background information, premises, conclusions, and information of other types, and often it’s not obvious what roles the various parts of the passages play. So, what’s the key to determining what roles the statements in a Boldface stimulus play? The key is finding any conclusions that appear in the passage, and in particular, the main conclusion. The roles of all the statements in an argument tend to depend on how the statements are related to the conclusion of the argument.

item.title: 12.5.3 In Boldface Questions, the Answer Choices Provide Us with Powerful Clues
item.description: Lesson Content As you now know, each of the five answer choices for a Boldface Method of Reasoning question either provides a description of the role played by one bolded section or provides a description of the role of the first of two bolded sections followed by a description of the role played by the second bolded section. The good news is that these answer choices provide us with clear ways to eliminate incorrect answers.

item.title: 12.5.4 Common Characteristics of Boldface Questions that Make Choosing the Correct Answers Tricky
item.description: Lesson Content If the passages in Boldface questions were structured in the way in which the passages in typical Weaken the Argument questions are structured, with some background information, perhaps two premises, and a conclusion at the end, determining the roles of the sections of the arguments would be rather easy. Here are some of the reasons why correctly identifying the roles of statements in and choosing the correct answers to Boldface questions can be tricky:

item.title: 12.5.5 The conclusions are inconspicuous.
item.description: Lesson Content In most of the Critical Reasoning arguments that we have seen, the conclusions have appeared at or near to the ends of the passages, and the fact that they are conclusions has often been signaled by words such as “Therefore” or “Clearly.” This pattern does not hold for most arguments seen in Boldface questions. 💡MUST KNOW: Often, in the arguments used in Boldface questions, main conclusions are not preceded by conclusion signaling words like “therefore,” and the conclusions are worded so as to be barely noticeable.

item.title: 12.5.6 The bolded portions can be worded and positioned to look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play.
item.description: Lesson Content Another way in which the creators of Boldface questions trick test-takers by taking advantage of common perceptions of how arguments work is by making the boldfaced portions look as if they play roles other than the roles that they play. 💡MUST KNOW: In the passages in Boldface questions, the portions in boldface can be worded and positioned to appear to play roles other than the roles that they play.

item.title: 12.5.7 The passages often contain multiple conclusions, along with premises to support the conclusions.
item.description: Lesson Content Another factor that can make sorting through the statements in a Boldface question and keeping everything straight challenging is the presence of multiple conclusions and the premises that support them. Differentiating between premises and conclusions. Determining which conclusions are supported by the arguments and which ones the arguments oppose. Determining which premises support which conclusion. Consider the following example: There is a common perception that, because higher than normal water temperatures can kill off coral reefs, and because climate change related emissions are causing increases in ocean water temperatures, other than reducing climate change causing emissions, there is not much that can be done to save reefs.

item.title: 12.5.8 Incorrect answer choices often say things that are partly correct or closely related to what is correct.
item.description: Lesson Content As we just discussed, sorting out the roles of the statements in Boldface question passages can be complicated by the fact that there can be multiple conclusions with premises that support each of them. For instance, if a portion in boldface is a premise that supports a conclusion opposed by the main conclusion of the argument, an answer choice that says that that boldfaced portion is itself a conclusion opposed by the conclusion of the argument could be a tempting trap.
